changelog.change: Standardized symptom labels: 12 symptom ids (fever, chills, sore_throat, swollen_lymph_nodes, runny_nose, sneezing, low_fever, nausea, dizziness_standing, itchy_eyes_nose, mucus_cough, recent_cold) were shared across multiple conditions with inconsistent display text for the same id. Unified each to one canonical label (shorter/simpler in most cases; 'Fever over 100.4°F' kept for fever as the more informative option). Ids, weights, and scoring untouched — labels only.
changelog.evidence: Caught by a new automated test asserting every symptom id resolves to a single label in the catalog. Not a scoring or safety issue — the intake picker only ever showed the first-seen label per id (getSymptomCatalog is first-label-wins), so some conditions' own labels were previously unreachable from the picker.

changelog.change: Added per-symptom severity (1-10) support: marked head and chest body regions as sensitive. Severity >= 8 on a sensitive-region symptom escalates urgency one tier (capped at urgent_care). Severity never affects condition scoring or red-flag logic.
changelog.evidence: Feature decision, not outcome data. Sensitive regions chosen because chest/head symptoms have the worst downside when under-triaged.

changelog.change: Raised loss_of_appetite weight in mononucleosis from 1 to 2.
changelog.evidence: 2 of 2 confirmed mono reports included loss of appetite; both missed the 40% threshold (36%). Small sample — revisit.

changelog.change: Initial knowledge base: 30 conditions with weighted symptoms, global and per-condition red flags, body regions.
changelog.evidence: Baseline — weights set from common clinical presentations, not outcome data.

_schema.note: Each condition has weighted symptoms. The triage engine sums weights for symptoms the user reports, divides by max_score to get a match percentage, and ranks conditions. Red flags are checked FIRST and override everything.
_schema.versioning_note: Bump `version` and append a `changelog` entry whenever weights or conditions change. Changes are decided MANUALLY by reviewing the /insights dashboard — never automated. See README 'Learning loop' for the workflow.
_schema.weight_guide: 3 = hallmark symptom (strongly suggests this condition), 2 = common symptom, 1 = supporting symptom (nonspecific)
_schema.matching_rule: A condition should only appear in results if the user reports at least one weight-3 symptom for it OR total score >= 40% of max_score
_schema.sensitive_regions_note: A body region with sensitive:true means: any reported symptom from that region rated >= 8/10 severity escalates result urgency one tier (capped at urgent_care, like all answer-based bumps). Severity NEVER changes match scoring and NEVER touches red-flag logic.
_schema.body_regions_note: body_regions drive the intake UI (plus the sensitive flag above). Each region lists symptom ids; labels are resolved from the conditions that use them. A symptom used by a condition but not listed in any region is automatically shown under 'General / Whole body' by the app, so new conditions can be added without touching code.
_schema.derived_flags_note: Some condition_red_flags ids are not user-selectable symptoms; the engine derives them from follow-up answers: fever / fever_over_104 (fever question), symptoms_over_3_days, symptoms_over_10_days, cough_over_3_weeks (duration question), recent_injury (injury question); head_injury_recent stays a selectable symptom under the Head region. Red flags that are never reported are still shown on the results screen as 'seek care immediately if...' warnings.

global_red_flags.label: Chest pain or pressure
global_red_flags.action: Call 911. Chest pain can signal a heart attack even in young people.

global_red_flags.label: Severe difficulty breathing
global_red_flags.action: Call 911 or go to the ER immediately.

global_red_flags.label: Face drooping, arm weakness, or slurred speech
global_red_flags.action: Call 911 immediately. These are signs of stroke and every minute matters.

global_red_flags.label: Thoughts of harming yourself
global_red_flags.action: You're not alone. Call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline) right now to talk to someone.

global_red_flags.label: Sudden, worst headache of your life
global_red_flags.action: Go to the ER. Sudden severe headache can indicate bleeding in the brain.

global_red_flags.label: Stiff neck with high fever
global_red_flags.action: Go to the ER. This combination can indicate meningitis.

global_red_flags.label: Throat tightness or face/tongue swelling
global_red_flags.action: Call 911. Rapid swelling can be anaphylaxis, a life-threatening allergic reaction.

conditions.name: Influenza (Flu)
conditions.description: A viral respiratory infection that hits fast and hard, usually with fever, body aches, and exhaustion.
conditions.advice: Rest, fluids, and fever reducers like acetaminophen. See a doctor within 48 hours of symptom onset if you're high-risk, since antivirals work best early. Most people recover in 5-7 days.

conditions.name: Strep Throat
conditions.description: A bacterial throat infection that needs antibiotics. Key clue: severe sore throat WITHOUT cough.
conditions.advice: See a doctor for a rapid strep test. Strep requires antibiotics — untreated, it can cause complications like rheumatic fever.

conditions.name: Common Cold
conditions.description: A mild viral infection centered in the nose and throat. Builds gradually, unlike the flu.
conditions.advice: Rest, fluids, and time. Saline rinses and honey (for cough) help. See a doctor if symptoms last more than 10 days or get worse after improving — that can signal a sinus infection.

conditions.name: Migraine
conditions.description: A neurological headache disorder, often one-sided and throbbing, with light/sound sensitivity or nausea.

symptoms.label: Visual aura (zigzags, flashing spots) before headache

conditions.advice: Rest in a dark, quiet room. OTC pain relievers work best taken early. If migraines are frequent (more than a few per month), see a doctor about preventive options.

conditions.name: Appendicitis
conditions.description: Inflammation of the appendix. Classic pattern: pain starts near the belly button, then migrates to the lower right abdomen.

symptoms.label: Pain that moved to lower right abdomen
Pain worse with walking, coughing, or bumps

conditions.advice: Go to urgent care or the ER today. Appendicitis worsens over 24-72 hours and a ruptured appendix is a surgical emergency. Do not eat or take laxatives while waiting to be seen.

conditions.name: Dehydration
conditions.description: Your body doesn't have enough fluid to function normally. Common after exercise, heat, illness, or not drinking enough.

symptoms.label: Dark yellow urine or urinating less

conditions.advice: Sip water or an electrolyte drink steadily rather than chugging. Rest in a cool place. If you can't keep fluids down or symptoms don't improve in a few hours, seek care.

conditions.name: Gastroenteritis (Stomach Flu)
conditions.description: Viral or bacterial inflammation of the stomach and intestines. Usually resolves on its own but dehydration is the main risk.
conditions.advice: Small sips of fluids with electrolytes. Ease back into bland foods (bananas, rice, toast). See a doctor if it lasts more than 3 days, or sooner if you see blood or can't keep any fluids down.

conditions.name: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
conditions.description: A bacterial infection of the urinary tract, most often the bladder. Needs antibiotics to fully clear.

symptoms.label: Strong urge but little comes out

conditions.advice: See a doctor for a urine test and antibiotics — UTIs rarely clear on their own and can spread to the kidneys. Fever with back or side pain means the infection may have reached the kidneys: get care today.

conditions.name: Panic Attack
conditions.description: A sudden surge of intense fear with strong physical symptoms that peaks within about 10 minutes. Feels dangerous but is not physically harmful.

symptoms.label: Feeling short of breath or smothered

conditions.advice: Slow your breathing: in for 4 counts, out for 6. Ground yourself by naming things you can see and hear. A first-ever episode with chest pain should be evaluated by a doctor to rule out heart issues. If attacks recur, a doctor or therapist can help a lot — panic disorder is very treatable.

conditions.name: Tension Headache
conditions.description: The most common headache type: a dull, band-like pressure on both sides of the head, often from stress, posture, or eye strain.

symptoms.label: Pain on both sides of head

conditions.advice: OTC pain relievers, hydration, and a break from screens. Stretch your neck and shoulders. If you're getting these more than twice a week, see a doctor rather than relying on daily painkillers, which can cause rebound headaches.

conditions.name: COVID-19
conditions.description: A viral respiratory infection. Symptoms overlap with flu and colds; loss of taste or smell is a distinctive clue.

symptoms.label: New loss of taste or smell

conditions.advice: Take a home test to confirm. Rest, fluids, and isolation per current guidance. People at high risk (older, immunocompromised, pregnant) should contact a doctor promptly — antiviral treatment works best started early.

conditions.name: Sinus Infection (Sinusitis)
conditions.description: Inflamed, blocked sinuses, often after a cold. Classic clue: cold symptoms that improve, then get worse again.

symptoms.label: Thick yellow or green nasal discharge

conditions.advice: Saline rinses, steam, decongestants, and time — most sinusitis is viral and clears in about 10 days. See a doctor if symptoms last beyond 10 days or come with high fever; that pattern suggests a bacterial infection.

conditions.name: Seasonal Allergies (Allergic Rhinitis)
conditions.description: An immune overreaction to pollen, dust, or dander. Itchiness and clear discharge distinguish it from a cold.

symptoms.label: Symptoms flare around triggers (outdoors, pets, dust)

conditions.advice: OTC antihistamines and steroid nasal sprays work well. Rinse sinuses with saline and keep windows closed on high-pollen days. See a doctor if OTC options don't control it — allergy testing and prescription options exist.

conditions.name: Asthma Flare-Up
conditions.description: Airway narrowing that causes wheezing, coughing, and chest tightness, often triggered by exercise, allergens, or infections.

symptoms.label: History of asthma or reactive airways

conditions.advice: Use your rescue inhaler as prescribed and sit upright. If you need your rescue inhaler more than twice a week, your asthma plan needs adjusting — see your doctor. Struggling to speak in full sentences means call 911.

conditions.name: Acute Bronchitis (Chest Cold)
conditions.description: Inflammation of the airways, usually after a cold. The cough can linger for weeks even after you feel better.

symptoms.label: Persistent cough lasting days to weeks

conditions.advice: Rest, fluids, honey for the cough, and a humidifier. Antibiotics usually don't help — most bronchitis is viral. See a doctor if the cough lasts over 3 weeks, or sooner with high fever or breathing trouble.

conditions.name: Pneumonia
conditions.description: An infection deep in the lungs. Sicker than a chest cold: high fever, shaking chills, and pain when breathing in.

symptoms.label: Sharp chest pain when breathing deeply

conditions.advice: Get seen today — pneumonia needs a chest exam and often antibiotics. It can worsen quickly, especially in older adults and people with lung conditions.

conditions.name: Middle Ear Infection (Otitis Media)
conditions.description: Fluid and infection behind the eardrum, common after colds. More frequent in children but adults get them too.

symptoms.label: Feeling of fullness in the ear

conditions.advice: See a doctor within a day or two — they'll check the eardrum and decide if antibiotics are needed. OTC pain relievers and a warm compress help in the meantime. Redness or swelling behind the ear needs same-day care.

conditions.name: Pink Eye (Conjunctivitis)
conditions.description: Inflammation of the eye's surface from viruses, bacteria, or allergies. Very contagious when infectious.

symptoms.label: Gritty, sandy feeling in the eye

conditions.advice: Wash hands often and don't share towels — it spreads easily. Most viral cases clear on their own; thick pus-like discharge may need antibiotic drops. Eye PAIN or vision changes are different: get seen the same day.

conditions.name: Acid Reflux (GERD)
conditions.description: Stomach acid flowing back into the esophagus, causing burning behind the breastbone, often after meals or when lying down.

symptoms.label: Worse when lying down or bending over
Sour or bitter taste in mouth
Food or liquid coming back up
Burning after large or spicy meals

conditions.advice: Smaller meals, avoid eating 2-3 hours before bed, raise the head of your bed, and try OTC antacids. IMPORTANT: crushing or pressure-like chest pain, especially with exertion, is treated as a heart problem until proven otherwise — call 911.

conditions.name: Food Poisoning
conditions.description: Illness from contaminated food, typically starting within hours of eating. Usually short and intense.

symptoms.label: Started within hours of a meal

conditions.advice: Small sips of electrolyte fluids, rest, and bland food when you can keep it down. Most cases resolve in 1-2 days. Blood in vomit or stool, signs of dehydration, or symptoms beyond 3 days mean get seen.

conditions.name: Constipation
conditions.description: Infrequent, hard, or difficult bowel movements. Usually from low fiber, low fluids, inactivity, or medication side effects.

symptoms.label: Fewer than 3 bowel movements a week
Feeling like you can't fully empty

conditions.advice: More water, more fiber (fruits, vegetables, whole grains), and a daily walk. OTC options like fiber supplements or gentle laxatives can help short-term. No stool AND no gas plus vomiting suggests a blockage — that's an emergency.

conditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
conditions.description: A chronic gut disorder with crampy pain and changed bowel habits, often flaring with stress or certain foods. Diagnosed by a doctor after ruling out other causes.

symptoms.label: Crampy pain relieved by a bowel movement

conditions.advice: See a doctor to confirm the diagnosis — IBS is diagnosed after ruling out other conditions. Blood in stool, weight loss, fever, or symptoms that wake you at night are NOT typical of IBS and need evaluation.

conditions.name: Kidney Stones
conditions.description: Hard mineral deposits passing through the urinary tract. Causes some of the most intense pain there is, coming in waves.

symptoms.label: Severe side/back pain coming in waves

conditions.advice: Get seen today for imaging and pain control. Many stones pass with fluids and medication, but size and position matter. Fever with stone pain can mean an infected, blocked kidney — that's an emergency.

conditions.name: Low Back Strain
conditions.description: Overstretched or torn back muscles, usually from lifting, twisting, or overuse. Painful but typically heals on its own.

symptoms.label: Low back pain that worsens with movement
Started after lifting, twisting, or activity

conditions.advice: Keep moving gently — long bed rest slows healing. Ice for the first day or two, then heat. OTC pain relievers help. Numbness in the groin area or losing bladder/bowel control are emergencies (cauda equina syndrome).

conditions.name: Ankle Sprain
conditions.description: Stretched or torn ankle ligaments, usually from rolling the ankle. Graded mild to severe by swelling and stability.

symptoms.label: Ankle pain after twisting or rolling it
Pain when putting weight on it

conditions.advice: RICE: rest, ice (20 minutes at a time), compression wrap, elevation. If you can't take 4 steps, the ankle looks deformed, or the foot is numb or cold, get an X-ray today.

conditions.name: Concussion
conditions.description: A mild traumatic brain injury from a blow or jolt to the head. Symptoms can appear right away or hours later.

symptoms.label: Recent blow or jolt to the head
Trouble remembering events around the injury

conditions.advice: Get evaluated today — concussions need a professional assessment and a return-to-activity plan. Someone should stay with you for 24 hours. Worsening headache, repeated vomiting, or unusual drowsiness → ER immediately.

conditions.name: Generalized Anxiety
conditions.description: Persistent, hard-to-control worry most days for months, with physical symptoms like tension, restlessness, and poor sleep.

symptoms.label: Excessive worry most days, for months
Feeling restless, keyed up, or on edge

conditions.advice: Anxiety this persistent deserves real help, and treatment works: therapy (especially CBT), lifestyle changes, and sometimes medication. Start with your doctor or campus counseling. If you ever have thoughts of self-harm, call or text 988.

conditions.name: Mononucleosis (Mono)
conditions.description: A viral infection (usually Epstein-Barr) common in teens and young adults. The signature is exhaustion that lasts weeks.
conditions.advice: See a doctor for a mono test. Rest is the main treatment — and AVOID contact sports for several weeks, because mono can enlarge the spleen. Sudden severe pain in the upper left belly could be a ruptured spleen: call 911.

conditions.name: Hives (Allergic Reaction)
conditions.description: Itchy raised welts from an allergic reaction — foods, medications, infections, or even stress and temperature changes.

symptoms.label: Itchy raised welts on the skin
Welts that appear, fade, and move around
Recently tried a new food or medication
Mild swelling of lips or eyelids

conditions.advice: OTC antihistamines usually calm hives within hours. Note what you ate or took beforehand. Hives PLUS throat tightness, trouble breathing, or dizziness is anaphylaxis: use an epinephrine auto-injector if available and call 911.

conditions.name: Heat Exhaustion
conditions.description: The body overheating from heat and dehydration. The step before heat stroke — cool down now to keep it from progressing.

symptoms.label: Heavy sweating during heat or exertion
Weakness or faintness after heat exposure

conditions.advice: Get to a cool place NOW, loosen clothing, sip cool fluids, and put cool wet cloths on skin. You should improve within an hour. Confusion or hot DRY skin means heat stroke — call 911 and cool aggressively while waiting.
